relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus approaches Jerusalem by the Mount of Olives.
The needed donkey and colt are already prepared.
The Lord’s need releases them.
The prophet’s word comes into embodiment.
Zion’s king comes gently, not as domination.
The disciples obey the instruction.
Garments become a throne-path for the gentle king.
The crowd spreads garments and branches on the way.
Hosanna rises around the Son of David.
Jerusalem is shaken by his arrival.
The crowd names him prophet from Nazareth of Galilee.
Jesus enters the temple and disrupts buying and selling.
The house of prayer has been turned into a cave of predation.
The blind and lame come into the temple and are healed.
Children praise while leaders are indignant.
Infants and nursing ones prepare praise.
Jesus leaves the city for Bethany.
Morning hunger leads to the fig tree sign.
Leaves without fruit are exposed.
The disciples marvel at the withered tree.
Undivided trust can move what seems immovable.
Prayer joined to trust becomes receiving.
Temple authorities question his authority.
Jesus answers with a question.
John’s baptism forces them to face heaven or human calculation.
Fear of the crowd exposes their politics.
Refusing to answer leaves them unanswered.
The two children parable tests words against action.
The first refuses, then turns and goes.
The second agrees, but does not go.
Doing the father’s will matters more than sounding obedient.
Those dismissed by religious status trusted John’s justice-way first.
The vineyard is planted, protected, and entrusted.
Fruit season arrives and servants are sent.
The tenants reject the servants.
More servants receive the same treatment.
Finally the son is sent.
The heir exposes the tenants’ desire to possess what is not theirs.
The son is cast outside and killed.
The question turns judgment back onto the hearers.
Fruitless tenants lose the vineyard to those who give fruit.
The rejected stone becomes the cornerstone.
The reign is given to a fruit-producing people.
The stone cannot be ignored without consequence.
The leaders understand the parables are about them.
They seek control but fear the prophet-honoring crowd.
